full_response:
Let x β(τ) be a geodesic written in the usual affine parameter τ, so that it obeys  

d²x β/dτ² + Γ β_{μν}(x) (dx μ/dτ)(dx ν/dτ) = 0 .  (1)

Now introduce a new parameter  

σ = a τ + b   (a,b = constants , a ≠ 0).                                 (2)

1.  Relating τ and σ  
 dσ/dτ = a  ⇒  dτ/dσ = 1/a (constant).  
 Therefore  

 d/dσ [ ln(dτ/dσ) ] = d/dσ [ ln(1/a) ] = 0 .  (3)

2.  Insert (3) into the general re-parametrised geodesic equation  

 d²x β/dσ² + Γ β_{μν}(dx μ/dσ)(dx ν/dσ)
                = (d/dσ) [ ln(dτ/dσ) ] (dx β/dσ).  (4)

Because the derivative on the right–hand side is zero, eq. (4) reduces to  

 d²x β/dσ² + Γ β_{μν}(dx μ/dσ)(dx ν/dσ) = 0 .  (5)

3.  Recovering the τ–form  
 Using the chain rule with (2),

 dx β/dσ = (dx β/dτ)(dτ/dσ) = (1/a) dx β/dτ ,  
 d²x β/dσ² = (1/a²) d²x β/dτ² .

 Substituting these into (5) and multiplying by a² gives precisely eq. (1):

 d²x β/dτ² + Γ β_{μν}(dx μ/dτ)(dx ν/dτ) = 0 .

Thus, when the new parameter σ differs from τ only by an affine transformation σ = aτ + b, the extra term in the general re-parametrised geodesic equation vanishes and we recover the familiar geodesic equation.

\boxed{\text{For }\sigma = a\tau + b,\; d/d\sigma\big[\ln(d\tau/d\sigma)\big]=0\;\Longrightarrow\;
d^{2}x^{\beta}/d\sigma^{2} + \Gamma^{\beta}_{\mu\nu}(dx^{\mu}/d\sigma)(dx^{\nu}/d\sigma)=0,
\;
\text{which is equivalent to the usual geodesic equation in }\tau.}

Confidence: 84%
